narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 27, your law school application stood out in some positive ways, like your solid college grades that are a bit above average and your upper-middle family income background, but the admissions model focused on key predictors of bar exam success. The model predicts a 72% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. This prediction hinges mostly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which was the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting failure—it's well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, signaling weaker performance in later undergrad years compared to peers. Coming in second was your undergraduate GPA; while your 3.30 is slightly stronger than the average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing, it wasn't enough to offset other concerns. Your family income bracket also nudged things positively toward a pass prediction, as upper-middle status aligns with students who often succeed, but again, it played a supporting role at best. Your low LSAT score of 27—far below the average of 36.7—likely amplified these issues indirectly, though it wasn't the top driver here, and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm for 93% of students. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having brighter spots in GPA and income, but your bottom-decile Year 3 performance made you riskier in the model's eyes, especially since strong late-stage grades are crucial for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a stable background—many in similar situations rebound strongly—but the model prioritizes patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, strengthening your later academic showings through grad courses or certifications, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your full-time commitment and upward trends. Schools value persistence, and targeted improvements could flip this prediction. You're clearly driven to pursue law, and with focused steps, you can align better with what models like this one reward.
